Claude.notes: Strong opening hook (camera/painting framing); concept sections answer narrative questions cleanly. The four-lesson arc (definition → training → architecture → capabilities/limits) is well-paced for Basic-level learners. Could use one returning character thread across lessons. No recurring character anchors the four-lesson arc; each lesson is self-contained which slightly weakens narrative continuity.
Claude.topIssue: Four-lesson arc lacks a recurring character or thread to anchor narrative continuity for Basic-level learners.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of generative AI, tokens, parameters, RLHF, attention, and context window are technically accurate at the appropriate depth for this audience. ChatGPT 5-day timeline, GPT-3 175B parameters, and 'Attention Is All You Need' citation are all correctly attributed. Module accepts published estimates for GPT-4 training cost ($100M+) and refers to GPT-4 size as 'significantly larger' than GPT-3 — appropriately hedged but worth flagging that exact GPT-4 parameters are speculative.
Gemini.topIssue: GPT-4 size and training cost figures are leak/estimate-based; module hedges appropriately but the figures should be flagged as estimates.

Perplexity.notes: Real cases (Mata v. Avianca lawyers June 2023, Thaler v. Perlmutter Aug 2023, Copyright Office rulings) are documented and current. Module dated April 2026. Context window table tops out at 'Claude 3.5 (2024) — 200,000 tokens' which is dated as of 2026 since newer Claude models have larger context windows. Recommend updating to current frontier specs.
Perplexity.topIssue: Context-window table topping out at Claude 3.5 (200K) is dated as of 2026 — frontier models now ship with significantly larger context.

Claude.notes: Word2Vec opening (king-man+woman=queen) is a strong concrete entry into prediction-vs-understanding. The CNET, WGA, and Levidow lawyer cases each set up the concept that follows. Lab 4 prompt-craft scaffolding (clarity/context/constraints) is excellent — but no single recurring narrator threads across the four lessons.
Claude.topIssue: No through-line character across the four lessons; each lesson's narrative anchor is self-contained.

Gemini.notes: Hallucination definition correctly distinguishes prediction from retrieval. WGA strike outcome (148 days; AI not 'literary material') is accurately summarized. CNET 77-article audit is correctly cited. Word2Vec analogy is from 2013 (Mikolov et al.) not 2016 — a minor attribution error worth correcting.
Gemini.topIssue: Word2Vec is attributed to 2016 but the original Mikolov paper is from 2013 — minor date error.

Perplexity.notes: Documented industry events (CNET 2023, WGA Sept 2023, Mata v. Avianca, Authors Guild) are well-grounded. Prompt engineering advice reflects current practice. References to '$300,000 prompt engineer salaries' reflect a brief 2023 hype peak; that market has cooled significantly. Module would benefit from a one-line update note.
Perplexity.topIssue: Reference to $300K prompt engineer salaries reflects a brief 2023 hype peak that has cooled materially.

Claude.notes: Diffusion explanation (start with noise, refine over 20-50 steps) is genuinely clarifying for the target age. Jason Allen → 'how does the AI actually do this?' → diffusion is a clean narrative-to-concept pivot. Lesson 3 (deepfakes) progresses naturally to L4 (creative tools). Hands/fingers as 'most reliable tell' is becoming dated as 2024-25 models have largely fixed hand artifacts.
Claude.topIssue: Hands/fingers as the 'most reliable tell' for AI images is increasingly outdated as 2024-25 models have largely solved hand rendering.

Gemini.notes: Diffusion process described correctly at conceptual level. Multiple real cases (Andersen v. Stability AI, Getty, Hong Kong fraud Feb 2024, LAION-5B) accurate. 'Hands and fingers' detection guidance is a documented but increasingly outdated heuristic; should be balanced with provenance/C2PA-style guidance.
Gemini.topIssue: Hand-anatomy heuristic for AI-image detection should be balanced with provenance/C2PA-style guidance — the visual heuristic is rapidly degrading.

Perplexity.notes: Eliot Higgins fake-Trump March 2023 incident, Hong Kong £20M deepfake fraud Feb 2024, Refik Anadol 'Unsupervised' at MoMA, Christie's Obvious 2018 sale — all current and verifiable. AI image detection guidance focused on hands is a 2023-era heuristic; 2025+ models produce convincing hands routinely. Recommend adding C2PA/provenance signals.
Perplexity.topIssue: Visual-detection guidance is from 2023; 2025+ image models render hands convincingly. Recommend updating with provenance/watermark discussion.

Claude.notes: Sony Flow Machines / Daddy's Car opening is concrete and grounded. Three building blocks (melody/harmony/rhythm) is a clear scaffold for music-theory novices. Holly Herndon framing in L4 ties practitioner ethics to consent. Lab 1 ('Try Suno') depends on a free tier that may not be reliably free; lab can complete via offline fallback but user agency in the actual music-tool would strengthen D5.
Claude.topIssue: Lab 1 'Try Suno' suggestion depends on a free tier that may not always be available; the offline fallback covers it but a tool-agnostic alternative would strengthen the lab.

Gemini.notes: Daddy's Car (2016, 13,000 lead sheets, Benoît Carré) accurately attributed. WaveNet 2016, MuseNet 2019, Suno/Udio 2023 timelines correct. Heart on My Sleeve attribution to ghostwriter977 and UMG takedown response is correct. 'Hi-Fi Rush (2023, Bethesda)' should credit developer Tango Gameworks (Bethesda is publisher); a small attribution error.
Gemini.topIssue: Hi-Fi Rush is credited to Bethesda, but the developer is Tango Gameworks (Bethesda is the publisher).

Perplexity.notes: RIAA/UMG/SME/WMG suit against Suno and Udio (June 2024) is accurately current. Holly Herndon's PROTO album (2019) is correctly attributed. 'Hi-Fi Rush (2023, Bethesda)' — attribution error: Tango Gameworks developed the game; Bethesda is the publisher.
Perplexity.topIssue: Hi-Fi Rush attribution: developer is Tango Gameworks, not Bethesda (which is the publisher).

Claude.notes: Greg Rutkowski opening is the right concrete anchor for the consent argument. Four-lesson arc (consent → lawsuits → ownership → policy/future) progresses cleanly. Lesson density is high; younger learners may struggle with the legal terminology layer. Vocabulary calibration drops a notch for the youngest end of the 13-18 audience.
Claude.topIssue: Vocabulary density spikes in the legal-terminology sections (class action, derivative work, fair use four-factor test); slightly above the floor of the 13-18 audience.

Gemini.notes: Andersen v. Stability AI (Jan 2023; Judge Orrick Oct 2023 partial dismissal), Getty v. Stability AI (Jan 2023 UK + Feb 2023 US), Authors Guild v. OpenAI (Sept 2023), Books3 'shadow library' references, Thaler v. Perlmutter Aug 2023 ruling — all correctly attributed and dated. Module's legal accuracy is strong. Minor: Books3 was scraped from Bibliotik, not generic 'piracy sites' — a small precision issue.
Gemini.topIssue: Books3 is described as scraped from 'piracy sites' generically; the documented source is the Bibliotik tracker — small precision issue.

Perplexity.notes: Coverage of EU AI Act (2024), NO FAKES Act, TRAIN Act (introduced 2024), Shutterstock-OpenAI / Getty-Nvidia / AP-OpenAI deals all reflect 2023-2024 reality. Several lawsuits described as 'ongoing' have moved substantially since 2024 (some settled, some have specific rulings). A 'last verified' note would help readers locate current status.
Perplexity.topIssue: Several lawsuits described as 'ongoing' have moved since the writing date (some partial rulings, settlements, dismissals). Module needs a 'status as of' note.

Claude.notes: Hollie Mengert case is a fresh and powerful narrative hook (vs the more-cited Rutkowski case). 'Voice vs technique vs style' framing is one of the cleanest conceptual lifts in the course. Lab 1 (Voice Mapping) and Lab 4 (Personal Policy) deliver real applied outcomes — D5 is the strongest in this module.
Claude.topIssue: No major issue — this is the strongest applied module in the course.

Gemini.notes: Glaze (Univ. of Chicago, Ben Zhao's group, 2023) accurately attributed. ArtStation 'Do Not Train' tag (Dec 2022) is correctly described. Adobe Firefly licensed-data approach is correctly framed. Could mention Nightshade (the offensive companion to Glaze, also from Zhao's group) released early 2024 for completeness on the technical countermeasures discussion.
Gemini.topIssue: Module discusses Glaze but does not mention Nightshade (Univ. of Chicago, early 2024), the offensive companion that meaningfully extends the same defensive toolkit.

Perplexity.notes: Lab outputs (Voice Map, Platform Audit, Personal AI Policy, 5-Year Plan) are genuinely actionable in today's AI landscape. Tools referenced (Glaze, ArtStation, DeviantArt) are current and available. Could update with Nightshade (2024) and SynthID/C2PA developments to keep the protection-tool inventory current.
Perplexity.topIssue: Protection-tool inventory misses Nightshade (2024) and emerging C2PA/SynthID provenance standards.

Claude.notes: Sunspring (2016, Oscar Sharp + Benjamin/Ross Goodwin) is a strong opening but the module then largely re-uses cases already covered in M3-M5 (Jason Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, Andersen lawsuit, Zarya, Christie's). Narrative density of new material is thinner than other modules. Three-models framing (Amplifier / Provocateur / Co-Author) is the original conceptual contribution — strong. But case repetition weakens narrative novelty.
Claude.topIssue: Module recycles cases already covered in M3-M5 (Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, Andersen, Zarya, Christie's); narrative-density-per-new-concept is the lowest in the course.

Gemini.notes: Sunspring screenplay attribution to 'Benjamin' (Ross Goodwin's LSTM) is correct. Three-models framing is supported by HCI literature. Heavy reliance on cases previously cited (Jason Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, Andersen, Zarya, Christie's) means the module is more of a recap than new content; technical claims are accurate but not adding much beyond M3-M5.
Gemini.topIssue: Cases are accurate but largely redundant with M3-M5; module's technical contribution is mostly the three-models framing.

Perplexity.notes: Cases cited are still current and verifiable. The Robin Sloan custom language model framing is well-grounded. Module relies on cases (Allen 2022, Heart on My Sleeve April 2023, Christie's 2018) that were already covered earlier in the same course. Less new applied learning per minute than other modules.
Perplexity.topIssue: Cases are correctly cited but already used elsewhere in the course; less new applied content per lesson than peer modules.

modules.module: M8: The Future of Creative Work

Claude.notes: WGA strike opening + photography historical parallel is a strong frame. The 'practical framework' closing (make things without AI first, document process, find community) lands as a real takeaway. Several cases reused from earlier modules (WGA, Heart on My Sleeve, Zarya, Adobe Firefly). For a capstone this is partly defensible — but the module also ends on advice rather than a final lab that demonstrates synthesis.
Claude.topIssue: Capstone reuses cases (WGA, Heart on My Sleeve, Zarya, Firefly) and ends with advice rather than a synthesis lab that asks the learner to integrate the full course.

Gemini.notes: McKinsey 2023 generative AI report findings, Adobe State of Creativity (83% figure), Adobe-College Board AP partnership, MIT Media Lab tools — all correctly attributed. 'McKinsey ~30% of creative tasks automatable by 2030' is in McKinsey's 2023 report band but is on the optimistic end of the cited range; should hedge.
Gemini.topIssue: McKinsey 'up to ~30% of creative tasks automatable by 2030' is on the optimistic end of the report's range and should be hedged.

Perplexity.notes: WGA contract specifics (148 days, AI not 'literary material'), Adobe 83% generative AI use within 2 years (2023 survey), AP Art curriculum integration — all current. 5-Year Creative Plan lab is the strongest applied outcome of the module, but the case foundation is largely retrospective; capstone could include a more current frontier case (e.g., 2025 SAG-AFTRA voice/likeness developments).
Perplexity.topIssue: Module would benefit from at least one 2025-frontier case (e.g., SAG-AFTRA voice/likeness developments, recent Suno/Udio rulings) to refresh its currency for a capstone.
